metaTitle: Freeze Header Row and First Column Online | {{BRAND_NAME}}
metaDescription: Keep your header row or first column visible while scrolling large tables — exactly like Excel freeze panes, directly in your browser. Free, no signup.
heroHeadline: Keep your headers in view no matter how far you scroll.
heroSubtext: Freeze the header row, the first column, or both. Scroll through a 50-row table and always know what every cell means — your labels stay pinned.

benefits.heading: Always know where you are
benefits.body: Scroll down 40 rows and the header row stays visible at the top. Scroll right and the first column stays in place. You never lose context in a large table.

benefits.heading: On and off in one click
benefits.body: Freeze header row and freeze first column are independent checkboxes. Turn either on or off instantly — no restructuring, no reformatting.

benefits.heading: Freeze both simultaneously
benefits.body: Enable both options together for tables that need row and column labels. The top-left corner cell stays fixed while the rest of the table scrolls freely.

benefits.heading: Freeze state is visual only
benefits.body: Frozen panes are a viewing aid. They do not affect your data, cell structure, or export. PDF, Excel, and PNG exports render the full table without the freeze behaviour.

steps.heading: Open the Header Definitions panel
steps.body: Find the Header Definitions section in the left sidebar. The Freeze options are at the bottom of this panel.

steps.heading: Check Freeze header row
steps.body: Tick the Freeze header row checkbox. The first row becomes sticky — it stays at the top of the table area as you scroll down.

steps.heading: Check Freeze first column (optional)
steps.body: Tick Freeze first column to also keep the leftmost column visible as you scroll right. Both options can be active at once.

useCases: Reviewing a 40-row research data table where the column headers must always be visible
Editing a 15-column table where the row labels in the first column identify each entry
Building a comparison table where both the category column and the header row need to stay fixed
Entering data into a long schedule or inventory table without losing track of which column is which
